stem: Which combination produces conventional T1 weighting in a spin-echo acquisition?

options.text: Long TR and long TE
Short TR and short TE
Long TR and short TE
Short TR and long TE

explanation: A short TR preserves differences in longitudinal recovery, while a short TE minimizes competing T2 decay. This combination makes T1 differences the dominant source of contrast.

stem: Which combination produces conventional T2 weighting in a spin-echo acquisition?

options.text: Short TR and short TE
Long TR and short TE
Long TR and long TE
Short TR and long TE

explanation: A long TR minimizes T1 saturation differences, and a long TE allows tissues with different T2 values to separate. Long-T2 tissues retain more transverse signal at the echo.

stem: Which combination best emphasizes proton density in a spin-echo acquisition?

options.text: Short TR and short TE
Long TR and long TE
Short TR and long TE
Long TR and short TE

explanation: A long TR reduces T1 weighting and a short TE reduces T2 weighting. With both relaxation effects minimized, signal differences more closely reflect the number of mobile hydrogen protons.

stem: Why does a short repetition time increase T1 weighting?

options.text: The next excitation occurs before tissues have fully recovered longitudinal magnetization
It allows all tissues to complete longitudinal recovery
It increases transverse decay by delaying the echo
It removes differences in spin-lattice recovery

explanation: With short TR, tissues are re-excited at different points in their T1 recovery curves. Short-T1 tissues have recovered more and therefore generally produce more signal than long-T1 tissues.

stem: Why does a long echo time increase T2 weighting?

options.text: It forces all tissues to recover longitudinal magnetization
It permits greater separation based on the rate of transverse decay
It eliminates spin-spin interactions before signal collection
It makes proton density the only remaining contrast source

explanation: A long TE delays sampling so short-T2 tissues have lost much more transverse magnetization than long-T2 tissues. The resulting signal difference emphasizes T2 properties.

stem: Why is a short-TR, long-TE spin-echo combination generally avoided for basic contrast weighting?

options.text: It removes both T1 and T2 contrast completely
It is mathematically identical to proton-density weighting
It introduces competing T1 and T2 effects that can oppose one another
It prevents formation of any spin echo

explanation: Short TR creates T1 weighting while long TE creates T2 weighting, and the tissue effects may pull signal in opposite directions. Standard protocols instead choose parameter pairs that emphasize one contrast mechanism cleanly.
